## Running[¶](#running "Permalink to this headline")

Please note that the following resource limits apply:

On CPU, SL1 and SL2 users are limited to 4256 cores in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job. On GPU, SL1 and SL2 are limited to 64 GPUs in use at any one time and a maximum wallclock runtime of 36 hours per job.
SL3 users are similarly limited to 448 cores (CPU) and 32 GPUs (GPU), all with up to 12 hours per job.
For more information, please see this full description of [service levels (SLs)](/hpc/user-guide/policies.html#service-levels).

Please see the example job submission scripts under `/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM`. There are example scripts for launching an MPI application on CPU or GPU via the queueing system:

```
/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-cclake

/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-icelake

/usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.wilkes3
```

Copying the appropriate example file and then modifying the top section (where indicated) will create a script suitable for submission to the batch queueing system via the command sbatch.

There are also examples of submission scripts for specific applications in this documentation - see for example [CASTEP](/hpc/software-packages/castep.html) or [LAMMPS](/hpc/software-packages/lammps.html).

Peta4 and Wilkes3 operate the SLURM batch queueing system for managing resources. Some useful commands:

```
squeue      -> show global cluster information
sinfo       -> show global cluster information
sview       -> show global cluster information
scontrol show job nnnn -> examine the job with jobid nnnn
scontrol show node nodename -> examine the node with name nodename
sbatch      -> submits an executable script to the queueing system
sintr       -> submits an interactive job to the queueing system
srun        -> run a command either as a new job or within an existing job
scancel     -> delete a job
mybalance   -> show current balance of core hour credits
```

Once your application is compiled, e.g. to a binary called *prog*, it can be submitted to the queueing system as follows (we assume it is destined for Ice Lake).

Firstly, copy the template SLURM submission script:

```
cp /usr/local/Cluster-Docs/SLURM/slurm_submit.peta4-icelake slurm_submit
```

(Note that for convenience newer users may have symbolic links to these template files in their home directories - these are read-only so making a copy is still necessary.)

Edit the copy slurm\_submit, setting application to “prog” and workdir to the correct working directory. Set options to contain any desired command line options, e.g “>outfile 2>errfile” would redirect stdout and stderr to files which could be monitored while the job runs. Note the comment lines at the head of the script:

```
#! Which project should be charged:
#SBATCH -A CHANGEME
#! How many whole nodes should be allocated?
#SBATCH --nodes=1

user_input:
Hello,
Is there any chance we can add a module for the last version of blast? I think the last one available is 2.11, and it is not complete (for example blastp is missing)
Thanks.
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software Request / Module Availability
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting a newer BLAST module because the currently visible version appears incomplete. The correct initial helpdesk response is to treat this as a software request: check whether a newer supported module already exists and, if not, investigate whether one can be added.

ACTION
1) Check whether a newer supported BLAST module already exists.
2) If not, investigate the request as a software-installation/update request.
3) If a newer module becomes available, send the exact module name and load command to the user.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello,

We can investigate whether a newer BLAST module can be provided.

If we are able to add a newer supported version, we will send you the exact module name and load command.

Best wishes,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Module Management
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is requesting the addition of a new module for the latest version of BLAST. The current version available is 2.11, which is missing some components like `blastp`. Previous tickets indicate that newer versions of BLAST have been built and tested, but they are not visible to users by default [1][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the availability of the latest BLAST module.
2. If available, make the module visible to users.
3. If not available, escalate to the software team to build and deploy the latest BLAST module.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the specific version number of the latest BLAST release you are referring to?
- Are there any specific features or components (e.g., `blastp`) that you need in the new version?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your request. We are aware that the current BLAST module (version 2.11) is missing some components like `blastp`. We will check the availability of the latest BLAST version and make it accessible to users if it is already built. If not, we will escalate this to our software team to build and deploy the latest version.

Please let us know if you have any specific version or features in mind that you need.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that any new module deployment follows the standard procedures and testing protocols.
- Communicate any changes or updates to the user promptly.

'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '# Modules Environment[¶](#modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

Software on CSD3 is primarily controlled through the *modules* environment. By loading and switching modules you control the compilers, libraries and software available.

When compiling or running programs on CSD3 you will need to set up the correct modules, to load your compiler and any librarys that are requierd (e.g. numerical libraries, IO format libraries).

Additionally, if you are compiling parallel applications using MPI
(or SHMEM, etc.) then you will need to load one of the MPI environments
and use the appropriate compiler wrapper scripts.

By default, the rhel/default-{system-name} module is loaded, depending
on the login node you used, which loads a set of other modules.

Basic usage of the `module` command on CSD3 is covered below. For
full documentation please see:

**Note:** The modules provided by the [Spack](http://spack.readthedocs.io)
package manager behave differently to those usually encountered in Linux
environments. In particular, each module has the versions of dependency
libraries hardcoded using RPATH. More information is provided below. You
can identify Spack modules as they have a random string of 7 characters at
the end of their name, e.g.: `boost-1.66.0-intel-17.0.4-2xrjal4`.

## Using the modules environment[¶](#using-the-modules-environment "Permalink to this headline")

### Information on the available modules[¶](#information-on-the-available-modules "Permalink to this headline")

Finding out which modules (and hence which compilers, libraries and
software) are available on the system is performed using the
`module avail` command:

```
[user@system ~]$ module avail
```

This will list all the names and versions of the modules available on
the service. Not all of them may work for your account however, due to,
for example, licencing restrictions. You will notice that for many
modules we have more than one version, each of which is identified by a
version number. One of these versions is the default. As the
service develops the default version will change.

### How to redirect module output[¶](#how-to-redirect-module-output "Permalink to this headline")

If using the BASH shell you may redirect standard error (**stderr**) to standard output (**stdout**) using the `2>&1` syntax. This enables you to search for specific items in the available modules:

```
#The below command will list all available modules with the phrase 'python-3.6' in them
[user@system ~]$ module avail 2>&1 | grep -i python-3.6
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-23fr5u4
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-64u3a4w
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-vag3zpv
python-3.6.1-gcc-5.4.0-xk7ym4l
python-3.6.2-gcc-5.4.0-me5fsee
python-3.6.2-intel-17.0.4-lv2lxsb
```

### Identifying currently loaded modules[¶](#identifying-currently-loaded-modules "Permalink to this headline")

The simple `module list` command will give the names of the modules
and their versions you have presently loaded in your envionment:

```
[user@login-e-11 ~]$ module list
Currently Loaded Modulefiles:
1) slurm                          6) turbovnc/2.0.1                11) intel/impi/2017.4/intel       16) intel/bundles/complib/2017.4

Leon Bond commented:
Dear Maja,
I believe that the version of blast we have on the cluster is too old (v2.4) to make secure connections to the remote site, i.e. https:// instead of http://
The blast documentation sugggests that v2.5+ is needed for this ([1][https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/](https://www.ncbi.nlm.nih.gov/books/NBK131777/))
We've built a v2.9 module of blast, which you can try by running
module load /usr/local/software/spack/current/share/spack/tcl/linux-scientific7-skylake_avx512/blast-plus-2.9.0-gcc-9.3.0-h4vn5dr 
That isn't currently visible to users by default, as we've had to use a different version of the 'spack' software packaging tool for this. In the near future we'll be making changes that will allow this to be visible via a search.
Could you try using this module and see if it works for you instead of blast/2.4.0 ?
Regards
Leon
Research Computing Services

### Loading, unloading and swapping modules[¶](#loading-unloading-and-swapping-modules "Permalink to this headline")

To load a module to use `module add` or `module load`. For example,
to load the pgi commpiler into the development environment:

```
module load pgi
```

This will load the default version of the pgi commpilers Library. If
you need a specfic version of the module, you can add more information:

```
module load pgi/2018
```

will load version 18.1 for you, regardless of the default. If you
want to clean up, `module remove` will remove a loaded module:

```
module remove pgi
```

(or `module rm pgi` or
`module unload pgi`) will unload whatever version of pgi (even if it is not the default) you might have
loaded.

## Private and group modules[¶](#private-and-group-modules "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to create private modules using the following procedure:

1. Create a folder named `privatemodules` in the home directory with:

```
cd ~
mkdir privatemodules
```

2. Add module files in the `privatemodules` folder
3. Access the private modules with:

```
module load use.own
```

This command can be added in the .bashrc file for accessing the private modules without entering the above command everytime the user logins.

3. To list the private modules is done using the `modules av` command. The private modules list will be shown below the other global module lists.

To create group modules a folder with the module files and has to be created in the rds folder that is available to all the members of the group. Moreover, the program files that the modules need must the rds storage too. Both of these folders and files containted must have read permission by all users.

Each user in the group must create a symbolic link to the folder with the group module files. An example of this command is shown below. Please change the path to the group modules to the absolute path to the folder with your group’s modules.

```
cd ~/privatemodules
ln -s /rds/my-groups-project/my-id/groupmodules/ groupmodules
```

user_input:
Dear Support Team,
I’m encountering an issue with Conda environment activation inside Slurm batch jobs on the cluster.
I have a Conda environment named ml that contains its own Python installation (Python 3.10). When I activate it interactively on the login node, it behaves correctly:
- conda activate ml
- which python → points to the env’s interpreter under .../envs/ml/bin/python
- python --version → Python 3.10.4
However, when I submit a Slurm batch job, the job still uses the base Conda Python:
Slurm job:
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake
1. Load your cluster's Anaconda/Miniconda module
2. (adjust to whatever your cluster uses)
module load miniconda
source .bashrc
1. Activate the environment
conda activate ml
1. Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"
1. Alternatively:
2. echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"
1. Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
Output:
Active conda environment: ml
Using Python from: /usr/local/software/master/miniconda/3/bin/python
What am I doing wrong here?
Kind regards,
Stefanos
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Slurm / Conda Environment Activation
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The batch job is falling back to the cluster-wide miniconda Python, even though the named environment appears active. The recommended fix is not to rely on the old shared miniconda module; instead the user should install their own Miniconda into `~/rds/hpc-work` and build environments there.

ACTION
1) Explain that the shared cluster miniconda install is old and can behave unexpectedly.
2) Instruct the user to install their own Miniconda in `~/rds/hpc-work`.
3) Recreate or move the environment there.
4) In job scripts, activate the personal environment rather than the shared module-based one.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Stefanos,

The issue is most likely caused by relying on the old shared miniconda install on the cluster. Please install your own Miniconda under `~/rds/hpc-work` and create your environment there instead.

That personal install will give you full control over the environment and should avoid the base-Python fallback you are seeing in Slurm jobs.

Relevant filesystem guidance:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/io_management.html

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Conda Environment Activation in SLURM
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is encountering issues with activating a Conda environment within a SLURM batch job. The Conda environment activates correctly on the login node but not within the SLURM job, which defaults to the base Conda Python. This issue has been documented in previous tickets and resolved by ensuring the `.bashrc` file is sourced and the correct Conda environment is activated [1][2][3][5][6][7].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's SLURM script to ensure it includes the necessary commands to source the `.bashrc` file and activate the Conda environment.
2. If the issue persists, consider suggesting the user install a local copy of Miniconda in their home directory or RDS workspace to manage their Conda environments independently [7].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you provide the exact SLURM script you are using?
- Have you tried sourcing the `.bashrc` file at the beginning of your SLURM script?
- Have you considered installing a local copy of Miniconda to manage your Conda environments?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Stefanos,

The issue you're experiencing with Conda environment activation in SLURM batch jobs is a known problem. To resolve this, please ensure your SLURM script includes the following commands:

```bash
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --job-name=conda-test
#SBATCH --output=conda_test.out
#SBATCH --error=conda_test.err
#SBATCH --time=00:05:00
#SBATCH -A KOURTZI-SL2-CPU
#SBATCH -p icelake

# Load the necessary modules
module load miniconda/3

# Source the .bashrc file to initialize Conda
source ~/.bashrc

# Activate the Conda environment
conda activate ml

# Print the active environment name
echo "Active conda environment: $(conda info --envs | grep '*' | awk '{print $1}')"

# Alternatively:
echo "Active environment via \$CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV: $CONDA_DEFAULT_ENV"

# Test Python activation
python -c "import sys; print('Using Python from:', sys.executable)"
```

If the issue persists, you may consider installing a local copy of Miniconda in your home directory or RDS workspace to manage your Conda environments independently.

Please let us know if you encounter any further issues.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user's SLURM script includes the necessary commands to source the `.bashrc` file and activate the Conda environment.
- If the issue persists, suggest the user install a local copy of Miniconda to manage their Conda environments independently.

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate` commands
to work.

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

Below we show a short example of creating a local Python environment and installing the

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

user_input:
Good morning,
I’d like to request your support as I’ve had issues setting up and using Python environments on CSD3.
Normally I used my own installation of micromamba and had set up environments with numpy, pytorch, etc. However, as of recently, launching python and running `import torch` freezes completely. I submitted a job consisting only of `python -c import torch` and it took over an hour to complete.
I cleaned up my local environment and completely uninstalled micromamba and cleared all caches.
Then, I attempted to follow the steps here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-packages/pytorch.html). However, the simple step of `python -m venv` took over 3 minutes to complete, indicating the problem is to do with python in general. I checked `which python` and it was using `/usr/local/software/spack/csd3/opt-2024-06-01/linux-rocky8-cascadelake/gcc-13.3.0/python-3.11.9-nptrdpllnmurec6o5hvu7iwyca5qgajy/bin/python`
Finally, I attempted to use the pre-installed miniconda3 per the instructions here: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/software-tools/python.html). However setting up a simple environment with pandas and pytorch has been running for several hours as of this email.
Can you help me figure out why it’s being so slow and what to do?
Could it be that the problem is with the file system? I attempted `python -m venv` both in my home directory and on hpc-work and it took very long in both cases.
Thanks for your help,
Stelios Boulitsakis Logothetis

The PyTorch [website](https://pytorch.org) describes the library as:

## Setup PyTorch on CSD3[¶](#setup-pytorch-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

The python installation will depend on the target partition (CPU or GPU).
To run Pytorch on a CPU partition (cclake, icelake), please do the following:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl # recommended for all RHEL8 CPU partitions (cclake, icelake, sapphire)
module load python/3.11.9/gcc/nptrdpll

# create and activate a virtual environment:

python -m venv ./pytorch-cclake-env
source ./pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env (instructions from Intel: https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/installation?platform=cpu&version=v2.7.0%2Bcpu&os=linux%2Fwsl2&package=pip)
python -m pip install torch torchvision torchaudio --index-url https://download.pytorch.org/whl/cpu
python -m pip install intel-extension-for-pytorch
python -m pip install oneccl_bind_pt --extra-index-url https://pytorch-extension.intel.com/release-whl/stable/cpu/us/
```

To run Pytorch on a GPU partition (ampere), please follow the following instructions:

```
#!/bin/bash

# load the relevant cluster environment and a recent python built for the cluster:
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp
module load python/3.8.11/gcc-9.4.0-yb6rzr6

# create and activate a virtual environment:
python -m venv ./pytorch-ampere-env
source ./pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# install pytorch into your virtual env:
pip install torch torchvision torchaudio
```

## Running a PyTorch program[¶](#running-a-pytorch-program "Permalink to this headline")

PyTorch will be able to run on both CPU and GPU.

CPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-CPU
#SBATCH --partition cclake
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#SBATCH --ntasks-per-node 1
#SBATCH --cpus-per-task 8
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-ccl

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-cclake-env/bin/activate

# Let PyTorch use all available CPU cores for OpenMP, MKL and MKL-DNN backends
export OMP_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK
export MKL_NUM_THREADS=$SLURM_CPUS_PER_TASK

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py
```

GPU submission script:

```
#!/bin/bash
#SBATCH --account MYACCOUNT-GPU
#SBATCH --partition ampere
#SBATCH --nodes 1
#! change to gpu:4 to use all 4 GPU cards on a GPU node.
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:1
#SBATCH --time 00:30:00
#SBATCH -o pytorch.out
#SBATCH -e pytorch.err

# Module setup: cluster environment and recent python.
module purge
module load rhel8/default-amp

# Activate relevant python virtual environment.
source pytorch-ampere-env/bin/activate

# Edit and replace 'your_script.py' with your own script
python your_script.py

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

### Using Anaconda Python in SLURM scripts[¶](#using-anaconda-python-in-slurm-scripts "Permalink to this headline")

Without running `conda init` the commands `conda activate` and `conda deactivate` will present
the following warning.

```
[user@login-e-15 ~]$ conda activate
CommandNotFoundError: Your shell has not been properly configured to use 'conda activate'.
To initialize your shell, run

    $ conda init <SHELL_NAME>

Currently supported shells are:
- bash
- fish
- tcsh
- xonsh
- zsh
- powershell

See 'conda init --help' for more information and options.

IMPORTANT: You may need to close and restart your shell after running 'conda init'.
```

Since `conda init` injects some logic into your .bashrc file it must be sourced. This is
not an issue for interactive shell sessions as it happens automatically when you login.
However this is not the case for SLURM jobs, therefore it is important to add `source .bashrc`
to your submission script to allow subsequent `conda activate` or `conda deactivate` commands
to work.

### Installing additional Anaconda Python modules[¶](#installing-additional-anaconda-python-modules "Permalink to this headline")

If the central `base` installation does not have a package or module that you require, you
can install this yourself by using *conda environments*.

A conda environemnt is a local copy of the central install that you can then modify
with additional modules/packages or even use different versions of existing
packages.

Full documentation on using conda environments can be found online at [Managing
conda environments](https://docs.conda.io/projects/conda/en/latest/user-guide/tasks/manage-environments.html).

*biopython* package.

## Use of AI Tools and Online Resources[¶](#use-of-ai-tools-and-online-resources "Permalink to this headline")

We understand that as AI assistants such as ChatGPT, Claude, and Gemini increase in popularity, our users may consult them for assistance with technical issues on our platforms. user_input:
Hi,
I am having an issue when debugging my Python code on the gfx nodes: when I try to run simple scripts which should finish in less than 1 second, they sometimes take 10s of seconds or even minutes in some cases to run.
When interrupting one of these scripts (with CTRL-C) the python traceback shows that the process was terminated when trying to import various modules.
The below terminal output from a very simple script where only a single module is imported is shown below:
[cid:a6653bb8-3762-4b4a-9dc4-5d29b74c798f]
As you can see, I ran the exact same command 3 times, with one of them taking substantially longer than the other two.
This issue seems to happen when more than a few minutes has passed since I last ran a script and is starting to become a real pain to my workflow as I when I make changes to code and want to test them I sometimes have to wait minutes for the script to run when it should take ~1 second.
To give some extra details as to my setup; I am using uv package manager with python 3.12.12. UV sets up a python virtual environment (venv) and installs dependencies there as is best practise.
If I had to guess as to what is going on, it seems to be some kind of file system latency where the venv files are normally in some kind of cache so are fast but after a while has passed they are removed and must be retrieved from somewhere much slower. Note that all of the python files and modules are stored in my RDS hpc-work folder.
Would you be able to look into this please? Perhaps there is some quirk of/issue with the lustre filesystem that is causing this?
Many thanks,
Leo
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Environment Performance / Shared Storage
Technical Action Required: No (performance guidance)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is seeing slow Python imports from a virtual environment stored on `hpc-work`. A likely explanation is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem rather than a Python bug, but support should first confirm there is no known wider incident before treating it as normal shared-storage behaviour.

ACTION
1) Explain that `hpc-work` is a shared RDS filesystem and can slow down when heavily loaded.
2) Check whether any cluster-wide incident is currently known.
3) Advise the user that environments on shared storage may show intermittent latency.
4) If the slowdown becomes persistent or widespread, request timings and node details for escalation.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

A likely cause is latency on the shared RDS-backed filesystem, since your virtual environment is stored in `hpc-work`, but we would first want to rule out any broader service issue.

If no wider incident is present, imports can sometimes be slower when the shared storage is under heavy load.

If this becomes persistent or starts affecting many commands consistently, please send timings and the exact node and location you are testing from and we can investigate further.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Software / Python
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is similar to known issues with slow Python library loading on the HPC system, particularly when using virtual environments stored in the RDS hpc-work folder [1][3][5]. This could be due to ongoing issues with the Lustre filesystem [1][3].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm if there are any ongoing issues with the Lustre filesystem that could be causing the slowdown.
2. Check if there have been any recent updates or changes to the Python environment or virtual environment setup that could be affecting performance.
3. If the issue persists, consider recommending the use of Anaconda as a potential workaround, as it has resolved similar issues in the past [5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you tried using Anaconda for your Python environment?
- Are you experiencing this issue on other nodes or only on the gfx nodes?
- Have you noticed any other unusual behavior or error messages?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Leo,

Thank you for reporting this issue. We are aware of similar problems with slow Python library loading, particularly when using virtual environments stored in the RDS hpc-work folder [1][3][5]. This could be due to ongoing issues with the Lustre filesystem.

We are currently investigating the issue and will let you know if we find a solution. In the meantime, you might want to try using Anaconda for your Python environment, as it has resolved similar issues in the past [5].

Please let us know if you have any other questions or concerns.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not perform manual changes to the filesystem or Python environment without proper investigation and confirmation.
- Ensure that any recommended workarounds, such as using Anaconda, are documented and have been successful in similar cases [5].

We support several different classes of interactive use, to allow code development, debugging, job monitoring or post-processing.

## Login nodes[¶](#login-nodes "Permalink to this headline")

Most simply, one can run programs straightforwardly on the command line of a login node. This is frequently sufficient for the purposes of compilation and job preparation. Note that provided you have an X server on your local machine, and you enable X-forwarding in your SSH connection (e.g. the -X or -Y options to ssh, see [Connecting to CSD3 systems](connecting.html)), then X-windows applications launched on a login node should display on your screen (but NB, **never** try to use *xhost* to make this work).

The login nodes also support VNC and X2Go remote desktops. In particular, post-processing of large data sets may involve interactive visualisation software using 3D graphics, which can be performed using VNC on the graphical `login-gfx` login nodes. Use of VNC with and without 3D is described in [Connecting to CSD3 via TurboVNC](turbovnc.html).

The login nodes are similar in terms of hardware to the cluster compute nodes. It is possible to run small MPI jobs on the login nodes for testing purposes using shared memory. However, the login nodes are finite, shared resources and any such use must respect other users. In particular, parallel test runs must be short (i.e. seconds), use no more than four CPUs and 20GB of memory each, and should be niced (prefixed with **nice -19**) so as not to impact interactive responsiveness. The login nodes are not to be used to run production workload outside the batch queueing system; antisocial use of a login node will quickly annoy other users and trigger the *watchdog* enforcement script.

Note that interactive use of *compute nodes* is available as [described below](#sintr).

## Job monitoring[¶](#job-monitoring "Permalink to this headline")

The progress of any batch job started through the scheduler can be monitored simply by logging in (using *ssh*) to any of the compute nodes assigned to the job (use e.g. squeue to see which these are). For example, the UNIX command *top* will immediately show whether there are active job processes, and what percentage of a CPU core each such process is managing to use; low percentages usually suggest a problem. Also from *top* one can verify the amount of memory per node that the job actually demands (see also the *free* command); exhausting the node memory will at minimum cause the node to start writing memory pages to swap (thus causing immediate and drastic performance degradation).

The following limitations apply to compute node access:

* Access is possible only via the login nodes (not directly from external machines, although it is possible to jump through a login node, see for example the **-J** option to *ssh* in recent OpenSSH versions).
* SSH access is granted to a user when a node starts running jobs owned by that user. If a user has multiple jobs on the same node, the SSH session will be associated with the most recently started job.
* Access will be revoked when the user’s job finishes.'}, {'rank': 7, 'doc_id': 'HPCSSUP-98486::chunk::0000', 'score': 0.015625, 'source': 'tickets', 'retrieval_sources': ['tickets'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 4, 'dense_score': 0.7019125, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'ticket_memory', 'authority_tier': 1, 'validity_status': 'current', 'freshness_hint': '2026-02-02T10:23:58.098+0000', 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': [], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': ['Python'], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [{'entity_id': '20a96d6643ba5068', 'entity_type': 'software', 'canonical_name': 'Python', 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-tools/python.html', 'doc_title': 'Python', 'source_scope': 'local_official', 'status': 'current', 'match_methods': ['alias_scan'], 'matched_aliases': ['Python', 'python'], 'matched_versions': []}], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.015625, 'source_ranks': {'tickets': 4}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': '[TICKET_SUMMARY] Nodes very slow

CSD3 provides central installations of both Python 2 and Python 3. The central
installation provides some of the most common packages used for scientific
computation and data analysis and additional packages can be added by users by
using either virtual environments or conda environments.

Recent versions of the Python interpreter can be accessed as python/2.7,
python/3.5, python/3.6, python/3.7 or python/3.8. The default is currently
python/3.6. These packages will be automatically upgraded to the latest point
release.

## Using Virtual Environments[¶](#using-virtual-environments "Permalink to this headline")

Virtualenv provides a method for installing new or upgraded Python packages as
a user without the need to ask support to make changes centrally. It is
currently supported in all the Python modules.

### Step-by-step guide[¶](#step-by-step-guide "Permalink to this headline")

We have installed virtualenv into the centrally available Python modules and it
is also available for the native Python installed as part of Scientific Linux 7
on CSD3. If you are happy to use the latter (which is version 2.7.5) there is
no need to load a Python module, otherwise, please load the desired Python
module first.

A guide to using virtualenv can be found here : <https://pypi.Python.org/pypi/virtualenv>.

In short, you can create a sandboxed version, in the directory of your choice
(and if does not exist you must create one first) e.g. YOUR\_PYTHON, of Python
via:

```
virtualenv YOUR_PYTHON
```

Then activate this via:

```
source YOUR_PYTHON/bin/activate
```

and deactivate via:

```
deactivate
```

You can get it to inherit the central packages via:

```
virtualenv --system-site-packages YOUR_PYTHON
```

Once the virtualenv environment is activated, use the normal methods for
downloading and installing Python packages (e.g. pip) and the packages will be
installed into your YOUR\_PYTHON directory, where they will override the
contents of the central Python installation. Invoke Python as normal and the
new components should be visible (as long as the virtualenv environment is
activated).

### Additional notes[¶](#additional-notes "Permalink to this headline")

If you are not in the location of the filesystem where `YOUR_PYTHON` is
present, you can use a full path instead of a relative path (e.g.
`/home/my-crsid/YOUR_PYTHON)` to activate the virtual env from every location
of the filesystem.

The command `virtualenv` must be done only once to create and initialize the
sandbox. After that, you just have to activate and deactivate accordingly to
your need.

## Using Anaconda Python[¶](#using-anaconda-python "Permalink to this headline")

To setup your environment to use the Anaconda distributions you should use:

for Python 2:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/2
```

or for Python 3:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
```

You can verify the current version of Python with:

```
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ module load miniconda/3
[user@login-e-17 ~]$ python --version
Python 3.7.4
```

user_input:
Dear support team,
I am trying to log in to my RCS storage (rcs-cez22-zielinski) using FileZilla. I am following the instructions given in "Reseach Storage Documentation”. Unfortunately, I am denied access after entering my password and the TOTP verification code (see screenshot).
I can succesfully log in to [https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/](https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/), so I assume I have the correct password. I have also succesfully set up MFA/TOTP for CSD3 (see screenshot), and I am correctly entering the code when prompted to do so.
Maybe I need a different password that is specific for this service?
Kind regards
Christina
Prof. Dr. med. Christina Zielinski
Professor of Infection and Immunity
Department of Pathology
University of Cambridge
Tennis Court Road
Cambridge CB2 1QP
cez22@cam.ac.uk
s[Screenshot 2026-03-08 at 09.42.33.png]
[IMG_5398.PNG]
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RCS Access / Group Membership / MFA
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The access failure is unlikely to be explained by a separate service-specific password, especially since the user can log in elsewhere successfully. The most likely next steps are to check whether there is an access-control/group-membership problem and to confirm that the user has generated the correct MFA token for RCS/CSD3 access via `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

ACTION
1) Check whether the account has the correct storage/access-group membership.
2) Instruct the user to create the correct TOTP via:
   `ssh <crsid>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
3) Share the MFA documentation.
4) Remind the user of the normal login path and storage path once access has been confirmed.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hi Christina,

You should not need a separate password for this service. We will check your storage access permissions on our side.

You will also need to create the MFA token that works with RCS/CSD3 access. Please run:
`ssh cez22@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the on-screen instructions. Guidance is here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk

Best wishes,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Issues [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Technical Action Required: No [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Escalation Required: No [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]
Urgency: Medium [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is attempting to log in to RCS storage using FileZilla but is being denied access after entering the correct password and TOTP verification code. This issue is likely due to the user not having set up the correct TOTP for SSH to CSD3 [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Direct the user to follow the instructions for setting up TOTP for SSH to CSD3 as outlined in the documentation [4][6].
2. Ensure the user has completed the steps to log into the special MFA node (multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk) and has scanned the QR code or entered the secret key into their authenticator app [4][6].
3. Verify that the user has followed the correct steps to log in to RCS storage using FileZilla [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Have you completed the steps to log into the special MFA node (multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk) and scanned the QR code or entered the secret key into your authenticator app?
- Have you followed the instructions for setting up TOTP for SSH to CSD3 as outlined in the documentation?
- Have you tried logging in to RCS storage using FileZilla after completing the TOTP setup?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Christina,

It appears that you may not have set up the correct TOTP for SSH to CSD3. Please follow the instructions for setting up TOTP as outlined in the documentation: [https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html).

Once you have completed the TOTP setup, please try logging in to RCS storage using FileZilla again. If you encounter any issues, please let us know.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure the user has completed the TOTP setup for SSH to CSD3 before attempting to log in to RCS storage using FileZilla [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].
- Do not provide the user with a different password for this service [1][2][3][4][5][6][7][8].

## How do I reset my TOTP for SSH to CSD3?[¶](#how-do-i-reset-my-totp-for-ssh-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

If you need to reset your TOTP second factor, e.g. because you no longer have access to your original phone, please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
The TOTP second factor is an important protection against user account compromise, and an identity check is therefore needed when a request to replace it is received. This is similar to the existing process for resetting University passwords in a context where face to face identity checks are not feasible most of the time.

When contacting the service desk to request a reset of MFA, please be sure to send the email from the email address we have registered against your user account.

The service desk agent will arrange a time at which to hold a brief video call with you at which you will be asked to present photographic proof of identity in the form of one of the following:

> * Passport
> * Driving licence
> * University card (for members of the University of Cambridge).

Expired forms of the above are acceptable as proof of identity. Following completion of this check, the TOTP secret will be reset and you will be able to revisit multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk to repeat the setup procedure.

* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

Mandatory MultiFactor Authentication (MFA) was introduced to CSD3 and storage services logins on 1st November 2022 (you can read the archived [full maintenance here](/hpc/full_maintenance-Nov2022.html)).

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are having trouble setting up your MFA, please consult the [FAQs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#frequently-asked-questions-faq) or watch our [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

## What does this mean?[¶](#what-does-this-mean "Permalink to this headline")

As well as your passwords and SSH keys, when you use the services, a *Time-based One-Time Password* (TOTP) code will be requested. This is a six digit number, refreshed every 30 seconds, which is generated typically by an app running on your mobile phone. Thus authentication will then require at least two “factors”, i.e. either password or SSH key (the first factor), plus TOTP (the second factor).

Two common such mobile apps (available from mobile app stores and tested by us) are *Google Authenticator* and *Microsoft Authenticator*, which indeed you may already have and be using for other services.

## What do I need to do?[¶](#what-do-i-need-to-do "Permalink to this headline")

There are two ways to access the CSD3 services, by SSH login and [login-web](/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html). You will need to set up separate authentication for each if you wish to use them.
This will mean you will end up with **two, different** TOTP codes; the TOTP created for use with **login-web** appears as **CSD3: your username** in your authenticator app, while the TOTP created for use with **SSH** appears as **CSD3: SSH Login** in your authenticator app

### I already use TOTP for other services[¶](#i-already-use-totp-for-other-services "Permalink to this headline")

If you already use TOTP to access other services, and have a mobile application supporting TOTP to enable its use, then you will be able to SSH to *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk* or login to the web site *https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk* and feed the QR code or secret key this generates into your existing application.

Please note that we have only verified this with Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator.

Follow [Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk).
Or follow [Walkthrough - Login-Web](#walkthrough-login-web).

### I do not use TOTP[¶](#i-do-not-use-totp "Permalink to this headline")

Firstly please install the mobile authentication app of your choice (we recommend Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator) onto your mobile phone, if you have no such application installed already.

Follow [Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk).
Or follow [Walkthrough - Login-Web](#walkthrough-login-web).

## Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

### Step 0: Watch our video guide[¶](#step-0-watch-our-video-guide "Permalink to this headline")

If you are new to SSH and terminal use, we highly recommend that you follow the [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

### Step 1: Connect to special MFA node[¶](#step-1-connect-to-special-mfa-node "Permalink to this headline")

When you are ready to configure TOTP, log into the special node *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk*, via a terminal (not a browser). The sole function of this node is to set up TOTP if you currently have none configured.

To access the special MFA node:

```
ssh <username>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The first time you connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (as for any new destination with SSH) you will see a message similar to this:

```
The authenticity of host 'multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (128.232.222.63)' can't be established.
ED25519 key fingerprint is SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so.
```

Please check that the fingerprint listed matches one of those listed as current at <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html>.

You may also see additional lines like the following:

```
This host key is known by the following other names/addresses:
    ~/.ssh/known_hosts:18: login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
    (4 additional names omitted)
```

This is to be expected as all login nodes on CSD3 share a single set of host keys. Provided the key fingerprint is current it is ok to respond **yes** to the final question:

```
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?
```

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. user_input:
Good evening,
I have recently been trying to connect to the CSD3 to start working in the cluster for the first time. I have Two-Factor Authentication on my account already enabled (please see image attached).
When I try to connect to the login node (login.hpc.cam.ac.uk) through ssh I am being requested to provide the TOTP Verification Code. Unfortunately, I did not retain the QR code when connecting for the first time to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Would it be possible to reset this multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk QR code so I can scan it with Google Authenticator to set the login for my account?
Many thanks for your assistance on this matter.
Martin
________________________________
From: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk <support@hpc.cam.ac.uk>
Sent: 27 January 2026 14:44
To: Martin Santamarina Garcia <ms3242@cam.ac.uk>
Subject: CSD3 systems at Cambridge (ms3242)
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cms3242%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C9ffee2c674c742b2c17608de5db28853%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639051218545595340%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=cM%2FDapcmf08FOqIdc0YID9mKhRhD%2BD4VW61nuNHb8Eg%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
Research Computing Services

CLASSIFICATION
Category: MFA Reset / SSH Re-enrolment
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user has lost access to the TOTP setup previously generated for cluster SSH login and needs the standard cluster MFA reset/re-enrolment path. After reset, they should SSH to `multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk` again to obtain a new QR code for cluster login.

ACTION
1) Confirm whether the user can transfer the existing authenticator setup from the old device; if not, follow the standard cluster MFA reset workflow.
2) Reset the user’s cluster MFA using the normal support process.
3) Instruct them to run:
   `ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`
   using their Cambridge password to obtain a new QR code.
4) Clarify that the web interface may prompt separately if web MFA also needs re-enrolment.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Martin,

We can reset the MFA for your cluster account and then you can re-enrol it.

Once the reset has been completed, please run:
`ssh ms3242@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk`

and follow the prompts to scan the new QR code into your authenticator app.

Best regards,
HPC Support

Subject:  CSD3 systems at Cambridge (bw450)   
Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
Peta4-CPU\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0 - Intel Skylake/Omni-Path, Cascade Lake/IB
\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0\xa0 and Ice Lake/IB CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU\xa0\xa0 - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR IB GPU cluster.
Please note that currently:
\xa0 Skylake and Cascade Lake use a RHEL7-compatible O/S
\xa0 Ice Lake and Wilkes3 use a RHEL8-compatible O/S
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
This combination of RHEL7 and RHEL8 will become pure RHEL8 in time.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk 
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA

\xa0\xa0\xa0 SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
\xa0\xa0\xa0 MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
\xa0\xa0\xa0 SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.
User environment
================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk. 
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see 
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.)](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C01%7Cbw450%40universityofcambridgecloud.onmicrosoft.com%7C86abf562d39b49259de308daf7c9e82b%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C638094743795512858%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJWIjoiMC4wLjAwMDAiLCJQIjoiV2luMzIiLCJBTiI6Ik1haWwiLCJXVCI6Mn0%3D%7C3000%7C%7C%7C&sdata=5Y5YvT%2BehC1Fw7mVs32Zt5jXi%2BZmpS3zbZb1xQfYoWk%3D&reserved=0.))
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU 
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
 
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
 
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
 
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
\xa0
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
 
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=\xa0 N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,
