'# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to either of the addresses below:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk will take you to one of a set of RHEL7,
OPA-enabled login nodes; these nodes are best suited to development
work for the RHEL7 Peta4 clusters. login-icelake will take you to a
different set of RHEL8, Mellanox OFED and CUDA-enabled login nodes
best suited for development work for Ice Lake and Wilkes3-GPU.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24

As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the

Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the best
snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create

================
CSD3 nodes currently run a mixture of RHEL7 and RHEL8-compatible
operating systems. We recommend that all application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on skylake* and cclake* partitions, please use
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk (aka login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk).
For development on icelake* and ampere* partitions, please use
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk.
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)
).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. The module names contain hashes uniquely
identifying the build options and dependencies but the modules can be
searched and located using the spack command. E.g. to search for
modules providing HDF5 1.10.1 compiled using GCC, search for existing
builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).

Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses SLURM for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
SLURM submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Skylake
-------
Skylake jobs require the partitions skylake or skylake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p skylake
or
#SBATCH -p skylake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
skylake partition provides 1 cpu and 6020MiB of RAM per task, and the
skylake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 12060MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 6020MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the Skylake nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 32 cores in total.
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake
-------------------------
Cascade Lake and Ice Lake partitions work in a similar way with the following
differences:
The cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3420MiB per task by default.
The cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task by default.
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3380MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by a
job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if it
rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
2240 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

## Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

### Step 0: Watch our video guide[¶](#step-0-watch-our-video-guide "Permalink to this headline")

If you are new to SSH and terminal use, we highly recommend that you follow the [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

### Step 1: Connect to special MFA node[¶](#step-1-connect-to-special-mfa-node "Permalink to this headline")

When you are ready to configure TOTP, log into the special node *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk*, via a terminal (not a browser). The sole function of this node is to set up TOTP if you currently have none configured.

To access the special MFA node:

```
ssh <username>@multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The first time you connect to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (as for any new destination with SSH) you will see a message similar to this:

```
The authenticity of host 'multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk (128.232.222.63)' can't be established.
ED25519 key fingerprint is SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so.
```

Please check that the fingerprint listed matches one of those listed as current at <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html>.

You may also see additional lines like the following:

```
This host key is known by the following other names/addresses:
    ~/.ssh/known_hosts:18: login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
    (4 additional names omitted)
```

This is to be expected as all login nodes on CSD3 share a single set of host keys. Provided the key fingerprint is current it is ok to respond **yes** to the final question:

```
Are you sure you want to continue connecting (yes/no/[fingerprint])?
```

### Step 2: Log into multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk[¶](#step-2-log-into-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have connected, you may see a password prompt to log in.

![multi login - access](../_images/multi-login.png)

**University of Cambridge users**: You will need to enter your University password.

**External users**: You will need to use your SSH key or your password, depending on how your account was created.

If TOTP is already configured for your account, a message will be printed and the connection closed:

![multi login - TOTP already configured](../_images/multi-noop.png)

Otherwise, a QR code and secret key will be printed.

![multi login - QR generated](../_images/multi-qr.png)

*Please be careful not to point your camera at the example QR code displayed on the web page, as it will happily read it but this code is not valid for any purpose. You want to use* **your** *QR code from* **your** *login session to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk.*

).
Environment modules loading additional software packages are now
being generated by spack. There are different spack package trees
for each node hardware type.
The module names contain hashes uniquely identifying the build options
and dependencies but the modules can be searched and located using the
spack command. E.g. to search for modules providing HDF5 1.10.1
compiled using GCC, search for existing builds using
spack find -v hdf5@1.10.1%gcc
which will list matching builds and also show which configure options
were on :plus: or off (~) . Less version information will show more
choices, e.g. one might start off with just spack find hdf5%gcc.
Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient
to search using the short hash form identifying each build (printed at
the start of line of output by the spack find -dvl command). E.g.
spack module find /i52euam
(notice the / introducing the hash).
Missing builds can be requested. (For more information about spack
see [https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)<http://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html](https://eur03.safelinks.protection.outlook.com/?url=http%3A%2F%2Fspack.readthedocs.io%2Fen%2Flatest%2Findex.html&data=05%7C02%7Cjb2421%40cam.ac.uk%7C82f7b6d03d904420a89e08de8e309829%7C49a50445bdfa4b79ade3547b4f3986e9%7C1%7C0%7C639104536533408285%7CUnknown%7CTWFpbGZsb3d8eyJFbXB0eU1hcGkiOnRydWUsIlYiOiIwLjAuMDAwMCIsIlAiOiJXaW4zMiIsIkFOIjoiTWFpbCIsIldUIjoyfQ%3D%3D%7C0%7C%7C%7C&sdata=NH%2F8TvUmdepzNnPbD3oQqKraOI1OcZKwSH4ANDOOC9Q%3D&reserved=0.)%3Chttp://spack.readthedocs.io/en/latest/index.html)>
Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Mandatory MultiFactor Authentication (MFA) was introduced to CSD3 and storage services logins on 1st November 2022 (you can read the archived [full maintenance here](/hpc/full_maintenance-Nov2022.html)).

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are having trouble setting up your MFA, please consult the [FAQs](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#frequently-asked-questions-faq) or watch our [video tutorial](https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OvTgniwVxEg&t=365s)

## What does this mean?[¶](#what-does-this-mean "Permalink to this headline")

As well as your passwords and SSH keys, when you use the services, a *Time-based One-Time Password* (TOTP) code will be requested. This is a six digit number, refreshed every 30 seconds, which is generated typically by an app running on your mobile phone. Thus authentication will then require at least two “factors”, i.e. either password or SSH key (the first factor), plus TOTP (the second factor).

Two common such mobile apps (available from mobile app stores and tested by us) are *Google Authenticator* and *Microsoft Authenticator*, which indeed you may already have and be using for other services.

## What do I need to do?[¶](#what-do-i-need-to-do "Permalink to this headline")

There are two ways to access the CSD3 services, by SSH login and [login-web](/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html). You will need to set up separate authentication for each if you wish to use them.
This will mean you will end up with **two, different** TOTP codes; the TOTP created for use with **login-web** appears as **CSD3: your username** in your authenticator app, while the TOTP created for use with **SSH** appears as **CSD3: SSH Login** in your authenticator app

### I already use TOTP for other services[¶](#i-already-use-totp-for-other-services "Permalink to this headline")

If you already use TOTP to access other services, and have a mobile application supporting TOTP to enable its use, then you will be able to SSH to *multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk* or login to the web site *https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk* and feed the QR code or secret key this generates into your existing application.

Please note that we have only verified this with Google Authenticator and Microsoft Authenticator.

Follow [Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk).
Or follow [Walkthrough - Login-Web](#walkthrough-login-web).

### I do not use TOTP[¶](#i-do-not-use-totp "Permalink to this headline")

Firstly please install the mobile authentication app of your choice (we recommend Google Authenticator or Microsoft Authenticator) onto your mobile phone, if you have no such application installed already.

Follow [Walkthrough - SSH to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk](#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk).
Or follow [Walkthrough - Login-Web](#walkthrough-login-web).

Welcome to the Cambridge Service for Data Driven Discovery (CSD3)
facility. CSD3 is comprised of distinct CPU and GPU systems:
CSD3-CPU      - Intel Cascade Lake, Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
HDR InfiniBand CPU clusters
Wilkes3-GPU   - NVIDIA A100/Mellanox HDR InfiniBand GPU cluster.
The O/S on CSD3 nodes is Rocky Linux 8, a rebuild of RHEL8,
where RHEL=Red Hat Enterprise Linux which is compatible
with CentOS, Scientific Linux and others but NOT Ubuntu.
For more information on the service please see:
docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk
Connecting
==========
Firstly, if you are an external user, please email a RSA SSH public
key to support@hpc.cam.ac.uk (please use at least 2048 bits and don't
forget to set a passphrase). The id of the user account is as given
on the Subject line to this email.
Internal Cambridge users, please use your Cambridge CRSid and
UIS password.
Before you login for the first time, please SSH to
multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk
to generate a TOTP MFA secret for your phone. multi.hpc has
the same host keys as the login nodes (see below). For more
information about MFA on CSD3 please see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html) .
All users should login via SSH to:
login.hpc.cam.ac.uk
This destination will take you to one of a set of login nodes which
may have either Cascade Lake or Ice Lake CPUs. If you prefer a
particular CPU type (e.g. because you are developing code to run on
that type) you may alternatively SSH to
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk or login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk to
reach nodes of that type.
We allow unrestricted SSH access to the CSD3 login nodes,
but have protected them with Fail2Ban. This means that after
repeated failed login attempts from the same origin, that origin
will be blocked from further access for 20 minutes.
The SSH host keys used by all login nodes have the following
fingerprints (which of these hashes you see will depend on your
client):
RSA
MD5:fd:5c:6b:7d:49:95:2f:da:7f:5c:50:9a:bb:ef:3f:24
SHA256:2rl+MXd9rsrDzFZwEItmhhiHTlLTIqN0d3TSGLTgjTI
ED25519
MD5:eb:e3:a1:f0:64:68:cf:9c:63:da:84:db:2e:ee:15:83
SHA256:nFVSXK+VRGCaUupQEdhXzO6kp01m2fzzmbgPr0sc2so
As well as SSH login, connection is possible via x2go and VNC (the
latter needs to be tunnelled over SSH).
Storage
=======
Each user has a hard quota of 50GiB on their home directory. This is
provided over NFS and hourly/daily/weekly snapshots are made
automatically which can be searched by the user. E.g. if you delete a
file by accident look in the hidden .snapshot directory and browse the
best snapshot for the file (which can be copied back to your home
directory).
The working/scratch storage has been implemented as part of the
Cambridge Research Data Storage (RDS) service. Please look under
~/rds/ for your personal "work" directory (it will have the name
hpc-work). There is currently a 1TB per user quota enforced on this
directory. Please note that it is possible to buy additional, larger
RDS areas, these are also visible on CSD3 under the ~/rds directory.
Data to be read by jobs (including software installations) and output
files from jobs should be placed in RDS areas, NOT the home
directory. This is because /home is NFS-based and lacks the
scalability of the lustre-based RDS. I/O to /home by jobs can create
global system issues.

================
CSD3 nodes currently run a common RHEL8-compatible operating system
but present additional development packages tuned to the node
hardware. We recommend that all optimised application binaries be
compiled on login nodes matching the target nodes as closely as
possible.
For development on cclake* partitions, the best choice is
login-cascadelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
On icelake* and sapphire* partitions, the best choice is
login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk .
Note that the login-icelake nodes do not have GPUs and have a different
(Intel) CPU type to the (AMD) ampere GPU nodes, so ampere development may
require interactive use of a GPU node (see
[https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr](https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/interactive.html#sintr)

which will list matching builds and also show which configure options

Full details of any matching builds including dependencies (e.g. which
MPI was used) can be extracted with
spack find -dvl hdf5@1.10.1%gcc@5.4.0
Existing builds have modules which can be identified with a command
such as
spack module find hdf5@1.10.1+mpi %gcc@5.4.0 ^openmpi@1.10.7
which returns the name of the corresponding module
(hdf5-1.10.1-gcc-5.4.0-i52euam). Note that it is sometimes convenient

Submitting jobs
===============
CSD3 uses Slurm for job scheduling. Please use the command
mybalance
to show your project, your current usage and the remaining balance in
compute unit hours.
On CSD3 the type of unit hour represented in mybalance output depends
on whether the project controls resources on a CPU partition (-CPU
projects) or a GPU partition (-GPU projects), i.e.
- projects ending in -CPU use CPU core hours
- projects ending in -GPU use GPU hours
The projects listed by mybalance are the projects you should specify in
Slurm submissions either through
#SBATCH -A YOUR_PROJECT
in the job submission script or equivalently on the command line with
sbatch -A YOUR_PROJECT ...
Cascade Lake
------------
Cascade Lake jobs require the partitions cclake or cclake-himem, i.e.
#SBATCH -p cclake
or
#SBATCH -p cclake-himem
and will be allocated the number of cpus required for the number of
tasks requested and a corresponding amount of memory. By default, the
cclake partition provides 1 cpu and 3410MiB of RAM per task, and the
cclake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6840MiB per task.
Requesting more cpus per task, or more memory per task, may both
increase the number of cpus allocated (and hence the charge). It is
more cost efficient to submit jobs requiring more than 3410MiB per task
to the -himem partition since more memory per cpu is available
there. Hyperthreading is disabled on the CPU nodes so there is no
distinction between cpus and cores - each node has 56 cores in total.
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids
----------------------------
Ice Lake and Sapphire Rapids partitions work in a similar way with the
following differences:
The icelake partition provides 1 cpu and 3370MiB per task by default.
The icelake-himem partition provides 1 cpu and 6760MiB per task by default.
Each node has 76 cpus.
The sapphire partition provides 1 cpu and 4580MiB per task by default.
Each node has 112 cpus.
Wilkes3-GPU
-----------
GPU jobs require the partition ampere (#SBATCH -p ampere) and may
request any number of GPUs per node from the range 1 to 4, which is
done via the directive
#SBATCH --gres=gpu:N
where 1 <=  N <= 4.
Each GPU node contains 4 NVIDIA A100-SXM-80GB GPUs, with 1000GiB RAM and
two AMD EPYC 7763 64-Core processors.
Any jobs requesting more than one node must request 4 GPUs per
node. Jobs less than one node in size will be prevented from
requesting more than 32 CPUs per GPU. The enforcement is performed by
a job submission filter which will produce an explanatory message if
it rejects a job outright.
The service levels defined for jobs are currently:
SL2:
4256 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
64 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 36 hours of wallclock time per job
SL3:
448 CPU cores per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
32 GPUs per user maximum for a maximum of 12 hours of wallclock time per job
In addition it is possible to submit low priority jobs of no more than
12 hours duration to a special project project-sl4-type (where
type=cpu or gpu) which allows a limited number of jobs to run when the
alternative is leaving nodes idle.
Please email support@hpc.cam.ac.uk if any problems are encountered.
Kind regards,

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

CSD3 has several login nodes, for reliability and to share the interactive workload. Also there are specific login nodes associated with each generation of hardware. For reasons of security it is necessary to use SSH in order to connect - any Linux distribution will contain the *ssh* client which can be used for interactive logins, and also *scp*, *sftp* and *rsync* which employ SSH natively for file transfers. If your Linux/MacOSX/UNIX machine does not have ssh, firstly please check your distribution for it (ensuring you get the most recent version available). Recent versions of Microsoft Windows also include the OpenSSH ssh command-line client as an optional feature (alternatively, PuTTY is often used on Microsoft Windows).

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

To use the icelake CPU nodes in particular - also suitable for the production GPU cluster (ampere GPU nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-icelake.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

Note that you may need to add the -X option to the above ssh command in order to enable transparent forwarding of X applications to your local screen (this assumes that you have an X server running on your local machine). The OpenSSH version of ssh sometimes requires that you use -Y instead of -X (try -Y if applications don’t appear or die with errors). These options to ssh direct the X communication through an encrypted tunnel, and should ‘just work’. In the bad old days before SSH the highly insecure method of persuading X windows to display on remote screens involved commands such as xauth and xhost - you do not and never will need to use either of these with SSH, and if you try to use them you may open up everything you type (including passwords) to be read, and even changed, by evil-doers - so *do not* try these methods, they are both redundant and dangerous.

Similarly scp, sftp or rsync should also be accepted, incoming to CSD3. Of course doing the same in an outward direction should also work, provided the other system does not block SSH.

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. user_input: During my interactive session with RStudio, I noticed that if I am not active on the page while my model is running, I am logged out of the RStudio server, but the login I use for the HPC does not work to log back into the RStudio server. I have attempted to refresh the HPC main page, log out and back in, and then relaunch my RStudio session that is currently running. However, when I try to relaunch it, I am redirected to an RStudio login page that displays the message: “Temporary server error, please try again.”  When I attempt to log in using the same credentials I use for the HPC, it does not allow me to log back in. I have also tried Chrome and Safari, and seem to run into the same login page. My models still run, but I would like to be able to access the R page while they are running so I can see its progress. Thank you!
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / RStudio Session Reconnect
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is trying to reconnect to an already running RStudio interactive session after the web page timed out. In this situation, the RStudio login prompt may require session-specific connection credentials rather than the user’s normal HPC password. The next step is to retrieve the connection details from the running session page and use the session password shown there.

ACTION
1) Direct the user to the page for the running session.
2) Retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there, including the password.
3) Use that session password at the RStudio login prompt instead of the normal HPC password.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hi Julia,

Please open the page for the running RStudio session and retrieve the session-specific connection details shown there.

Use the password provided on that session page at the RStudio login prompt rather than your normal HPC password.

If you still cannot reconnect, please send us a screenshot of the session page and the login prompt.

Best wishes,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Interactive Apps / RStudio
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues with RStudio sessions logging out and being unable to reconnect using their HPC credentials. This issue has been reported before and may be related to inactivity during the session [1][2].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Confirm the user's settings for starting an interactive RStudio session [1][2].
2. Advise the user to try reconnecting via the active sessions page [1].
3. If the issue persists, escalate to a higher support tier for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- What are the exact settings you are using to start the interactive RStudio session?
- How often does this issue occur?
- Have you tried using the default modules (R rstudio-server) without any additional modules?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello,

Thank you for your message. We understand that you are experiencing issues with RStudio sessions logging out and being unable to reconnect. This issue has been reported before and may be related to inactivity during the session.

Please try the following steps:
1. Go to the active sessions page: https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk/pun/sys/dashboard/batch_connect/sessions

### RStudio server: Csd3[¶](#rstudio-server-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is an open-source, integrated development environment (IDE) for R. It includes a console, syntax-highlighting editor that supports direct code execution, and tools for plotting, history, debugging, and workspace management.

#### Starting RStudio on CSD3[¶](#starting-rstudio-on-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

From the `Interactive Apps` dropdown, select `RStudio server: Csd3`. This should open a job form as shown in the following image:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-start.png)

Fill the job details, then press the button `Launch` at the bottom of the page. This will lead you to your job page.
When the job is starting, the page should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-connect.png)

Press the button `Connect to RStudio Server`. This will open the RStudio IDE in a new browser tab, which should look like this:

![RStudio IDE](../_images/ondemand-apps-rstudio-firsttime.png)

The IDE is made of panels and tabs. From the default tab `Console`, 3 panels are visible:

* The actual console, to the left, with a prompt to an interactive R session.
* A first management panel, on the top-right corner, to manage Environments, check your session history or the available tutorials.
* A second management panel, on the bottom-right corner, to manage files, plots, packages and look at plots.

#### Managing user environments and projects on RStudio[¶](#managing-user-environments-and-projects-on-rstudio "Permalink to this headline")

RStudio is a complete IDE that allows direct project creation and management.

To create a new project, select `File` from the top toolbar, then `New project..`, as shown below:

then follows the instructions from the pop-up wizard. It is good practice to create a new folder
for each new project. At the end, a setup file ending with *.Rproj* is created.

Use the console to add new packages to your project, as shown below:

## Installing R packages[¶](#installing-r-packages "Permalink to this headline")

While a few R packages have been centrally installed on CSD3 (do `module avail r-` to see a list), it is often necessary to install extra R packages into your own environment. For example to install cellranger, one can use:

```
$ R
R version...
[...]
> install.packages("cellranger")
```

which will download and build the package from CRAN.

If you run into build errors, the first fix is to tell R where to find the correct compiler. This can be done by creating the file `~/.R/Makevars` in your home directory and adding:

```
CC = gcc
CXX = g++
CXX11 = g++
FC = gfortran
F77 = gfortran
F90 = gfortran
```

which tells R to use the system compilers. If you run into problems with the compiler versions being too old, then try loading a more recent compiler before starting R. If you run into problems that refer to the C or C++ standard versions then try adding:

```
CFLAGS = -std=c99
CXXFLAGS = -std=c++11
```

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

Login with your CSD3 HPC credentials: CRSid and password.

![Login page](../_images/ondemand-login.png)

Once completed you will be presented with this page:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page.png)

Or this page if your browser window is more compact:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page-compact.png)

The three-lined icon to the right of the compact page expands when clicked to show the full toolbar available on the non-compact page. At this point, it is possible to access the tools available in Login-Web.

## File Transfer and Management[¶](#file-transfer-and-management "Permalink to this headline")

This function allows the transfer of data to and from [Research Storage Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store) project areas, or your CSD3 home directory. To use the file transfer client, select `Files` in the main menu.

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-menu.png)

Login-Web provides a web-based File Explorer that can be used to upload and download files to/from your CSD3 home directory or project directory, and copy, delete, rename, and edit files. Below you can see a screenshot of the main interface:

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files.png)

### Navigating[¶](#navigating "Permalink to this headline")

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-goto.png)

By default, the file browser will be looking at your home directory. If you have access to a RDS or RCS project space, browse to the rds or rcs subdirectories of your home directory as normal; alternatively if you know the full path to the directory you can move stepwise through the path near the top of the window. user_input:
Dear support team
I am having trouble accessing the RFS service for my lab group (TP group, Condensed Matter Physics, Maxwell Centre). It has already been mapped, and it was working fine, but today it asked me for the password of my username (of the computer I was using). As you know, those are generated tokens. So, I generated a new one for the computer I am using, but it does not accept it. How can we fix that? It is urgent that I have access to my data.
Best regards
Patricia N. Lazo Paravicini
Pilizota Lab
Department of Physics
University of Cambridge
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: RFS Access / Credential Confusion
Technical Action Required: No (workflow clarification)
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user was trying to use a generated token/device password for RFS access on Windows. The correct fix is to ensure they are on the Cambridge VPN or network, follow the Windows RFS instructions, and use their normal UIS credentials for Access Blue rather than the VPN/device token.

ACTION
1) Confirm the user is on the Cambridge VPN or directly on the network.
2) Direct them to the Windows RFS guide:
   https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html
3) Clarify that the username/password prompt is for normal UIS credentials, not the VPN/device token.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Patricia,

Please make sure you are connected to the Cambridge VPN or the Cambridge network directly, and follow the Windows guidance here:
https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/storage/rfs/accessing-rfs/windows-10.html

For this login prompt, you should use your normal UIS credentials rather than the generated VPN/device token.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / RFS Access
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: High

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user is experiencing issues accessing the RFS service despite having generated a new token. The problem might be related to network or VPN issues, as seen in similar cases [1][3][5].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Verify the user's network connection and VPN status.
2. Ensure the user is following the correct RFS access instructions [2].
3. If the issue persists, consider resetting the user's access to RFS [3][5].

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you confirm if you are connected to the CUDN or using a working VPN?
- Have you tried restarting your network connection or VPN?
- Can you provide a screenshot of the error message you are receiving?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Patricia,

Thank you for reaching out. It seems like there might be an issue with your network or VPN connection. Please ensure you are connected to the CUDN or have a working VPN. '# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. ## How do I reset my TOTP for SSH to CSD3?[¶](#how-do-i-reset-my-totp-for-ssh-to-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

If you need to reset your TOTP second factor, e.g. because you no longer have access to your original phone, please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk.
The TOTP second factor is an important protection against user account compromise, and an identity check is therefore needed when a request to replace it is received. This is similar to the existing process for resetting University passwords in a context where face to face identity checks are not feasible most of the time.

When contacting the service desk to request a reset of MFA, please be sure to send the email from the email address we have registered against your user account.

The service desk agent will arrange a time at which to hold a brief video call with you at which you will be asked to present photographic proof of identity in the form of one of the following:

> * Passport
> * Driving licence
> * University card (for members of the University of Cambridge).

Expired forms of the above are acceptable as proof of identity. Following completion of this check, the TOTP secret will be reset and you will be able to revisit multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk to repeat the setup procedure.

## Frequently asked questions (FAQ)[¶](#frequently-asked-questions-faq "Permalink to this headline")

* I am unable to login to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk and now I get “Connection refused”.

  > + Make sure you are using the same userid, password or public key as you would to login.hpc.cam.ac.uk. As with the login nodes, failing to authenticate three times in succession to multi will cause your address to be blocked for 20 minutes, so if this happens please wait this long before trying again.
* I am using MS Authenticator but the TOTP code doesn’t work.

  > + If you still have the QR code and secret key on your screen then delete the account in your Authenticator and recreate it, making sure that the account is of the **Other** type (not *Personal* or *Work*). Otherwise please contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again, and select account type **Other**.
* When performing ssh to multi.hpc.cam.ac.uk the QR code is replaced either by garbage or is broken up by blank lines, making it unusable.

  > + Setting **export LANG=en\_GB.UTF-8** in Mac terminals before performing the ssh may stop this happening - however if this has already happened you will need to contact *support@hpc.cam.ac.uk* to request reset of the MFA secret before trying again.
* Connection sharing doesn’t work with Filezilla and I keep seeing TOTP requests during file transfers.

  > + Please prefer to use the RDS gateway *rds.uis.cam.ac.uk* for file transfers. This will request TOTP less frequently.

You will now have access to the portal.

### **Identity of Last Resort - Accessing without MyAccessID**

If you are experiencing difficulties using MyAccessID because you cannot find your organisation within the system, it is crucial to take the necessary steps to address this issue. In such a case, you will need to request a local account from our service desk.

At the bottom of the page, you will find a support option. Please click on it and copy the email address: support@hpc.cam.ac.uk .

[![Keycloak_signin_support](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)](../_images/Keycloak_signin_support.png)

When composing your email, make sure to include the title “Identity of last resort on Cambridge Access Portal” in the subject line. This will help the support team to identify your request promptly.

Please provide the name of your organisation along with the email address that will be used to access the portal.

[![Support_email_draft](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)](../_images/Support_email_draft.png)

The service desk team will process your ticket and create your local account in our Keycloak system. You will receive an email with a link to setup your local account password from the service desk team.

Please click on the link provided in the email to set up your password.

[![Support_new_password](../_images/Support_new_password.png)](../_images/Support_new_password.png)

Now that you have the password, you can begin the authentication process from the project invitation link received from Cambridge Access Portal admin.

Please select “Sign in with Keycloak.”

[![Dawn_keycloak_signin](../_images/Dawn_keycloak_signin.png)](../_images/Dawn_keycloak_signin.png)

To login to the Dawn access portal, you need to provide your email address along with your newly setup password. It is important to ensure that the email you enter is identical to the one that was requested for the identity of last resort.

Furthermore, the password you use should be the one that you established by following the link that was shared with you by the service desk. This process is essential for maintaining security and ensuring that only authorised users can access the service desk functionalities.

Once you provide your local account credentials, please click on the “**Sign In**” button.

user_input:
To Whom It May Concern,
For context, I have been using the Remote Desktop interactive sessions via the web interface for about a year.  As of this morning, requesting a desktop no longer provides the “Launch Remote Desktop” button.  Please see the attached screenshot. I expected to see the “Launch Remote Desktop” button as shown in the second screenshot. I would appreciate any insights or support. Thank you.
-Pete
reference:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: OnDemand / Remote Desktop Launch Configuration
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The missing “Launch Remote Desktop” button can be caused by launch-form settings such as project or account selection, but the screenshots are needed to confirm the exact cause. The safest next step is to review the full launch settings and confirm which project or account is selected.

ACTION
1) Ask the user for a full screenshot of the launch settings if not already available.
2) Confirm that the correct project or account is selected in the Remote Desktop form.
3) If project selection is wrong, explain that the default project may not be appropriate.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Pete,

Please send us the full launch settings for the Remote Desktop session, including the selected project/account, so that we can confirm what is preventing the expected button from appearing.

If the current screenshot does not show the full launch form, please send one that includes the relevant project/account fields.

Best regards,
HPC Support
response:
CLASSIFICATION
Category: Remote Desktop / Login-Web
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: Possibly
Urgency: Medium

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The issue described is similar to previous cases where the "Launch Remote Desktop" button is missing or the session jumps to "completed" status without launching [1][3][5]. This could be due to various reasons such as missing desktop partition association, full user quotas, or software issues.

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Check if the user has the necessary desktop partition association for their project.
2. Verify if the user's home directory quota is full, which might prevent the launch of the remote desktop [1].
3. Review the OndemandPortal logs for any errors related to the user's session.
4. If the issue persists, escalate to the storage team for further investigation.

QUESTIONS TO ASK (ONLY IF NEEDED)
- Can you specify the project you are using when launching the remote desktop?
- Have you recently made any changes to your home directory or installed any new software?
- Can you provide the session ID for the failed remote desktop launch?

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Pete,

Thank you for reaching out. The issue you're experiencing with the "Launch Remote Desktop" button not appearing is similar to previous cases we've seen. This could be due to missing desktop partition association, full user quotas, or software issues.

To help us diagnose the problem, could you please specify the project you are using when launching the remote desktop? Additionally, have you recently made any changes to your home directory or installed any new software?

We will review the OndemandPortal logs and check your home directory quota to see if that is causing the issue. If the problem persists, we may need to escalate it to the storage team for further investigation.

Please provide the session ID for the failed remote desktop launch if available.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Ensure that the user's desktop partition association is correctly set up.
- Verify the user's home directory quota to prevent issues with launching remote desktops.
- Escalate to the storage team if the issue cannot be resolved through initial troubleshooting.

[3] : HPCSSUP-74649

### Remote Desktop[¶](#remote-desktop "Permalink to this headline")

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-select.png)

This option will launch a full remote desktop in a browser tab. Select `Remote Desktop` and you will arrive on the page shown below. Click `Launch`. You may need to wait for a few minutes for the application to launch.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-wait.png)

To access the desktop you can choose from `Launch Remote Desktop` to reach your desktop as shown below and interact with it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share your desktop with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-starting.png)
![Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-launch.png)

Once the desktop is ready to be used, your session manager will show a job which looks like the image below:

![XFCE Remote desktop](../_images/ondemand-remote-desktop-xfce.png)

The remaining options shown under `Interactive Apps` will use the same functionality to launch specific applications, rather than full desktops.

### MATLAB[¶](#matlab "Permalink to this headline")

MATLAB allows matrix manipulations, plotting of functions and data, implementation of algorithms, creation of user interfaces, and interfacing with programs written in other languages, including C, C++, C#, Java, Fortran and Python.

#### Accessing through Login-Web[¶](#accessing-through-login-web "Permalink to this headline")

All the desktop apps can be found within the `Interactive Apps` drop-down in the Login-Web web portal as shown in the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-menu.png)

After selecting MATLAB, you will arrive at this page:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-select.png)

Here you can select the resources you would like your MATLAB session to have using the menu on the right hand side of the page. If you’d like to receive an email notifying you of when this has happened, please select `I would like to receive an email when the session starts`. After clicking `Launch`, a new session will be queued. Once the session is active your page will look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab-launch.png)

To access the interface you can choose from `Launch MATLAB` to reach the application as shown below and use it normally, or `View Only (Shareable Link)` to share the application interface with colleagues in `View Only` mode.

#### Using MATLAB[¶](#using-matlab "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have entered your session, you should see a new tab. Allow MATLAB a moment to start up, then your screen should eventually look like the image below:

![MATLAB](../_images/ondemand-apps-matlab.png)

Once connected to the remote desktop, usage proceeds as described above in [Using 3D graphics](#using-3d-graphics).

To simplify launching, locating and terminating 3D remote desktops, there are three commands which can be run from any login node:

```
startgfx

showgfx

killgfx nodename:displaynumber
```

As suggested by the names, *startgfx* will launch a new remote desktop on a random member of the 3D-capable set of nodes, and print out helpful information such how to connect to and how to terminate the desktop.

Note that if this is the first time VNC has been launched by the user, they will be prompted to set one or more VNC passwords, as described in [Starting a new VNC session](#starting-a-new-vnc-session). startfx will also print the version of the vncviewer command line required to connect from Linux or MacOS as described above in [Connecting to an existing VNC session](#connecting-to-an-existing-vnc-session), but if using other clients manual configuration of a jump host or SSH tunnel will be necessary as described in [Manually creating a SSH tunnel](#manually-creating-a-ssh-tunnel).

startgfx will complain and decline to start a second desktop if it detects that the same node is already running one for the same user, as this is usually wasteful and unintentional.

*showgfx* will find and list any 3D desktops owned by the same user.

*killgfx* (or equivalently, *stopgfx*) will terminate the 3D desktop specified by its node name and display number.'}, {'rank': 5, 'doc_id': 'HPCSSUP-90144::chunk::0001', 'score': 0.015873015873015872, 'source': 'tickets', 'retrieval_sources': ['tickets'], 'retrieval_source_ranks': {'tickets': 3}, 'retrieval_signal_trace': {'dense_rank': 3, 'dense_score': 0.7494612, 'signal_type': 'dense'}, 'source_authority': 'ticket_memory', 'authority_tier': 1, 'validity_status': 'current', 'freshness_hint': '2025-08-01T16:09:09.957+0100', 'title': None, 'doc_title': None, 'system_names': [], 'partition_names': [], 'service_names': ['Login-Web Interface'], 'scope_family_names': [], 'software_names': ['Python'], 'software_versions': [], 'module_names': [], 'toolchain_names': [], 'toolchain_versions': [], 'official_doc_matches': [{'entity_id': '0854bea5159593d2', 'entity_type': 'service', 'canonical_name': 'Login-Web Interface', 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/login-web.html', 'doc_title': 'Login-Web Interface', 'source_scope': 'local_official', 'status': 'current', 'match_methods': ['alias_scan'], 'matched_aliases': ['login-web'], 'matched_versions': []}, {'entity_id': '20a96d6643ba5068', 'entity_type': 'software', 'canonical_name': 'Python', 'doc_id': 'https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/software-tools/python.html', 'doc_title': 'Python', 'source_scope': 'local_official', 'status': 'current', 'match_methods': ['alias_scan'], 'matched_aliases': ['Python', 'python'], 'matched_versions': []}], 'rerank_trace': {'reranker_type': 'rrf', 'rrf_k': 60, 'rrf_score': 0.015873015873015872, 'source_ranks': {'tickets': 3}, 'source_weights': {'docs': 1.0, 'tickets': 1.0}}, 'text': "[TICKET_SUMMARY] remote desktop launch issue

Login-Web is a “one stop shop” for access to our High Performance Computing resources. With Login-Web, it is possible to upload and download files, create, edit, submit, run GUI applications, and connect to nodes via SSH, all via a web browser, with no client software to install and configure.

## System Requirements[¶](#system-requirements "Permalink to this headline")

Currently, the site is confirmed to work with newer versions of Chrome (22+), Firefox (16+), and Microsoft Edge (11+). We are still working to expand availability to additional clients, and are planning on including compatibility for mobile devices (phones and tablets) in the future.

Note

When logging in for the first time to Login-Web, you will be required to set up MFA, please see the MFA documentation for more information: <https://docs.hpc.cam.ac.uk/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html>

If you need to reconfigure your MFA please contact support@hpc.cam.ac.uk and request a token reset.

### Connecting[¶](#connecting "Permalink to this headline")

To connect to Login-Web, visit <https://login-web.hpc.cam.ac.uk>. The first page of the Login-Web site redirects you to the login page.

Tip

For a video guide on how to use Login-Web for CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

### Logging in[¶](#logging-in "Permalink to this headline")

Login with your CSD3 HPC credentials: CRSid and password.

![Login page](../_images/ondemand-login.png)

Once completed you will be presented with this page:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page.png)

Or this page if your browser window is more compact:

![screenshot of Login-Web front page](../_images/ondemand-front-page-compact.png)

The three-lined icon to the right of the compact page expands when clicked to show the full toolbar available on the non-compact page. At this point, it is possible to access the tools available in Login-Web.

## File Transfer and Management[¶](#file-transfer-and-management "Permalink to this headline")

This function allows the transfer of data to and from [Research Storage Services](https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/research-data-store) project areas, or your CSD3 home directory. To use the file transfer client, select `Files` in the main menu.

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-menu.png)

Login-Web provides a web-based File Explorer that can be used to upload and download files to/from your CSD3 home directory or project directory, and copy, delete, rename, and edit files. Below you can see a screenshot of the main interface:

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files.png)

### Navigating[¶](#navigating "Permalink to this headline")

![Files menu open](../_images/ondemand-files-goto.png)

By default, the file browser will be looking at your home directory. If you have access to a RDS or RCS project space, browse to the rds or rcs subdirectories of your home directory as normal; alternatively if you know the full path to the directory you can move stepwise through the path near the top of the window. Important

The graphical visualisation nodes *gpu-e-1,…, gpu-e-7* retired on Monday 10th July 2023 and were replaced by new NVIDIA L40 nodes *gpu-r-1,…, gpu-r-10*.

## Starting a new VNC session[¶](#starting-a-new-vnc-session "Permalink to this headline")

The recommended form of remote desktop uses the freely downloadable software [TurboVNC](http://www.turbovnc.org/). This is because TurboVNC, which is centrally installed on CSD3, contains an improved codec for better transmission of rapidly varying 3D images. Hardware accelerated 3D (see below) will still work with other forms of VNC (or e.g. [X2Go](https://wiki.x2go.org/)), although in these cases the performance may be decreased depending on the codecs available for compressing and decompressing the desktop images.

Since 3D visualisation-capable graphics cards are only attached to a subset of nodes, in order to use 3D the TurboVNC server should be started on one of these nodes. Please see the [startgfx](#startgfx) section for instructions for launching and managing these specialised remote desktops, however the general descriptions of first-time password setting and the SSH-tunnel method of connecting to the remote desktop that follow remain applicable in the 3D case. You will probably wish to try creating a non-graphical remote desktop initially.

If you don’t plan to make use of 3D graphics, then you should launch a VNC session on an ordinary login node as follows.

To start a VNC session, first ssh/PuTTY in your usual way to a CSD3 login node. Obviously it is best to choose a relatively non-busy node (the commands top or w will display current system load). Then do:

```
vncserver
```

(where in the default environment this will refer to the correct version of VNC).

The first time you run this you will be asked to set a VNC password. Note that this is **not your login password** and should be set to something different (but still strong) - its purpose is to protect your remote desktop from access by other users who are already logged into the cluster. You will also be asked for a second, optional password which can be shared with others if you want to give someone else the ability to see, but not change, your remote desktop, e.g. to show them some simulation result - this need not be set. Once you have set a VNC password it will be stored in your home directory and you will not be asked to set it again (unless you delete the ~/.vnc/passwd file).

The vncserver command will start a VNC session on the next available display number. The name of the display will be reported by the vncserver command in the form hostname:N where N is the display number. You will need to note the display number, and also remember the hostname in order to connect to the correct desktop later.

Note that vncserver -list will list active sessions belonging to you which are running on the same node, and -kill can be used to terminate a specified display number (for the 3D desktops, the *showgfx* and *killgfx* special commands make this easy).

Hello,
I hope you're well.
I'm a current PhD student and was just made a Data user on the Research Data Store for the Genetics_HPC partition by a collaborator. Upon opening the authentication link, I completely mistakenly clicked "Do not agree", rather than "Agree". The latter was certainly my intention, and now cannot access the drive.
Would there be any way to reverse this and reauthenticate?

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage Access / Invitation Workflow
Technical Action Required: Yes
Escalation Required: No
Urgency: Low

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user accidentally rejected the storage access invitation by clicking “Do not agree”. Helpdesk cannot reverse that state directly; the storage/data manager who originally added the user must re-add or re-invite them.

ACTION
1) Explain that the original invitation cannot simply be toggled back by helpdesk.
2) Direct the user to the Data Manager or project owner who granted access.
3) Ask that person to re-add/reinvite the user to the storage.

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY

Hello Maria,

In this case the Data Manager who added you to the storage will need to re-add or re-invite you. We cannot reverse that response directly from the helpdesk side.

Best regards,
HPC Support

CLASSIFICATION
Category: Storage / Access Management [1][4][6]
Technical Action Required: No [1][4][6]
Portal Action Required: Yes (Self Service) [1][4][6]
Urgency: Medium [1][4][6]

QUICK ASSESSMENT
The user mistakenly clicked "Do not agree" to the terms and conditions for accessing the Research Data Store. This issue has been resolved in similar cases by having the data manager re-add the user to the project [1][4][6].

ACTION STEPS (HELPDESK)
1. Inform the user that the data manager needs to re-add them to the project to reset the access process [1][4][6].
2. Provide the user with the contact information for the Storage Services Team if further assistance is needed [1][4][6].

EXAMPLE CUSTOMER REPLY
Hello Maria,

Thank you for your message. To resolve this issue, please ask your data manager to re-add you to the project. This will reset the access process and allow you to accept the terms and conditions.

If you need further assistance, please contact the Storage Services Team at storage-services@uis.cam.ac.uk or (+)44 1223 763517.

Best regards,
HPC Support

SAFETY / POLICY NOTES
- Do not manually reset the access process without involving the data manager [1][4][6].
- Ensure the user is directed to the appropriate contact information for further assistance [1][4][6].

## PI Managing Projects[¶](#pi-managing-projects "Permalink to this headline")

**Understanding roles and permissions for Cambridge Research Computing Access Portal:**

There are three roles you can have on a project:

* Principal Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Co-Investigator
  :   + Can invite team members to the project to access the resources requested.
      + Can manage team members (add/remove) users from a project.
* Researcher
  :   + Can access and utilize project resources.
      + Cannot create new resources or invite team members.

The Principal Investigator (PI) is a special role that should be held only by the person (or people) who are legally responsible for the project.

In doing so, the PI confirms that they have conducted the required due diligence on the person they are inviting, and that they are happy to take responsibility for the actions of the person they invite.

### Inviting Users[¶](#inviting-users "Permalink to this headline")

Once you have successfully onboarded to the portal, you can begin accessing its features and functionalities.

The documentation below will cover inviting team members:

**Inviting someone to your project**

To invite someone to your project, click the “Projects” link in the left menu to access your projects.

'# Quick Start[¶](#quick-start "Permalink to this headline")

Important

* If you are a brand new internal user to the HPC service and have not submitted a HPC Account Application form, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/rcs-application>. Do not attempt to login without submitting the form.
* If you are a first time user OR have not used the system since **1st November 2022**, you will need to set up your MFA. Follow the [MFA guidance documentation](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk)

## First Time Accessing CSD3[¶](#first-time-accessing-csd3 "Permalink to this headline")

All users of the HPC service will be required to have MFA set up in order to access and use the services.

It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

For an introduction to CSD3, how it works, who can access and how, in video format, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Login[¶](#login "Permalink to this headline")

All users should contact CSD3 initially through the login nodes.

To access the CPU cluster nodes (for use with CPU compute nodes):

```
ssh <username>@login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk
```

Research Computing Services use CRSids where they exist and so the <username>@ can usually be omitted if your University of Cambridge system does also. Internal Cambridge users should use their UIS password (at least initially). SSH keys can also be used (but *always* set a passphrase).

The **first time** you login, you will be asked to check that the host key fingerprints are correct. Please check that the fingerprints reported match those on the page [CSD3 Host Keys](/hpc/user-guide/hostkeys.html) *before* responding *yes* (NB not *y*).

There are in fact multiple individual login nodes which may be accessed directly. Logins are balanced over `login-q-1` to `login-q-4` (icelake) and `login-p-1` to `login-p-4` (cascadelake).

Note that the name `login.hpc.cam.ac.uk` is an alias for `login-cpu.hpc.cam.ac.uk`, which is itself an alias for `login-csd3.hpc.cam.ac.uk`.

All CSD3 nodes run a rebuild of Red Hat Enterprise Linux 8 (RHEL8) and have the same access to filesystems. The nodes vary however in the generation of their CPUs. Therefore it is recommended to develop code on the closest matching type of login node, according to the choices suggested above, and certainly to recompile applications that were previously run on a different version of RHEL.

Tip

For a video and tutorial guide for getting started on CSD3, please visit <https://www.hpc.cam.ac.uk/getting-started-csd3-tutorials>

## Password[¶](#password "Permalink to this headline")

It is possible to change your initial password using the usual unix command *passwd* on a login node. University of Cambridge users should note that this will make it different to your UIS Password - see the UIS Password Management Application <<https://password.raven.cam.ac.uk/>> for changing the latter. It is strongly recommended that you read through the [MultiFactor Authentication (MFA)](/hpc/user-guide/mfa.html#walkthrough-ssh-to-multi-hpc-cam-ac-uk) user documentation to assist with the setup of your MFA.

Once you have submitted your SSH key and set up your MFA, you will be able to ssh to the Dawn login nodes.
All Dawn users should contact Dawn initially through the Dawn login nodes.

```

```
